claim: CS/CS/HJR 203 proposed a Florida constitutional amendment to exempt homestead property from all ad valorem taxation other than school district levies beginning in a specified year.
notes: The Senate bill page summary describes the proposed homestead exemption and non-school ad valorem scope.

claim: CS/CS/HJR 203 passed the Florida House by an 80-30 vote on February 19, 2026.
notes: The official bill history lists the House third-reading passage and the vote history lists 80 Yeas and 30 Nays.

claim: After House passage, CS/CS/HJR 203 was received by the Senate and referred to Senate Appropriations on February 19, 2026.
notes: The official bill history lists Senate receipt and referral to Appropriations.

claim: CS/CS/HJR 203 died in Senate Appropriations on March 13, 2026.
notes: The official bill page lists the last action as Senate - Died in Appropriations.

claim: The April 24, 2026 Senate memo described expected Special Session D legislation on congressional redistricting, artificial intelligence, public records, and medical freedom.
notes: The memo identifies SB 8D as anticipated redistricting legislation and lists SB 2D, SB 4D, and SB 6D as other Special Session D legislation.

claim: As of the April 28, 2026 Senate bill-actions page reviewed for this tracker, Special Session D bill actions listed H 1D, S 2D, S 4D, S 6D, S 8D, and H 23D, and no tracked property tax elimination proposal appeared on that list.
notes: This tracker treats the official bill-actions list and the April 24 Senate memo as the source of truth for the listed Special Session D items reviewed on April 28, 2026.

claim: The Florida Senate redistricting page describes Special Session 2026-D as centered on congressional redistricting materials and a plan transmitted by the Executive Office of the Governor.
notes: Used for context on Special Session D materials, not as a property-tax source.

claim: Florida special sessions may be called by the Governor or by joint proclamation of the Senate President and House Speaker.
notes: Used to explain why a later special session is a possible future vehicle.

claim: A Florida constitutional amendment can be proposed by legislative joint resolution.
notes: Used to describe the ballot path for a legislative property-tax amendment.

claim: A proposed amendment to the Florida Constitution requires at least 60 percent voter approval to pass unless the constitution provides otherwise.
